author: Alkarim Billawala / alkarim.billawala.ca
weeks: Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen

questions.stem: A 44-year-old with US-confirmed appendicitis is awaiting the OR when she becomes confused: HR 140, BP 80/45, O2 86% on oxygen, lactate 9. Best next step?
options: Aggressive resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent source control, and ICU/surgical escalation
questions.explain: This is septic shock with organ dysfunction from an intra-abdominal source. Resuscitate, give early broad antibiotics, achieve source control, and escalate to ICU/senior surgery. Waiting for perfect imaging in refractory shock is unsafe.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has LLQ pain, fever, and localized peritonitis. CT shows sigmoid diverticulitis with fat stranding, no abscess or free air. Best confirmatory test and management principle?
options: CT abdomen/pelvis; supportive care with selective antibiotics by severity and follow-up colonoscopy after recovery
Immediate sigmoid colectomy for all first episodes
questions.explain: CT is the best acute test for diverticulitis; colonoscopy is avoided acutely (perforation risk) but done after recovery (cancer mimics diverticulitis). Uncomplicated disease is managed supportively with selective antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman has severe RUQ pain, jaundice, fever, confusion, HR 125, BP 80/40. US shows gallstones and dilated intra- and extra-hepatic ducts. Diagnosis and treatment?
options: Acute cholecystitis; oral antibiotics and discharge
Ascending cholangitis; resuscitation, broad antibiotics, and urgent ERCP decompression
questions.explain: Fever + RUQ pain + jaundice + hypotension + altered LOC = Reynolds pentad = severe ascending cholangitis. Treat with resuscitation, broad antibiotics, and urgent biliary decompression by ERCP.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man has bright red blood dripping after straining and feels tissue prolapse that reduces spontaneously. Colonoscopy shows no neoplasm. Most likely diagnosis and grade?
questions.explain: Painless bright-red bleeding with prolapse that reduces spontaneously = grade II internal hemorrhoids. Grade I bleeds without prolapse; III needs manual reduction; IV is irreducible.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with a prior laparotomy has 24 h of crampy pain, bilious vomiting, distension, and obstipation. X-rays show dilated small-bowel loops; no fever, no peritonitis, normal lactate; CT shows a transition point without ischemia. Most likely cause and initial management?
options: Adhesions; NPO, IV fluids, electrolytes, NG decompression, close surgical observation
Ogilvie syndrome; neostigmine before excluding obstruction
questions.explain: Prior surgery → adhesive SBO (commonest cause). A stable, simple/partial SBO without ischemia is managed conservatively: bowel rest, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, NG decompression, and close observation.

questions.stem: A 43-year-old man with iron-deficiency anemia has a colonoscopy showing an ascending-colon mass; biopsy = adenocarcinoma. CT shows no metastases; CEA is sent. Most appropriate initial definitive treatment?
options: Rubber-band ligation because hemorrhoids explain the anemia
Oncologic right hemicolectomy with lymphadenectomy, adjuvant therapy by final stage
questions.explain: A biopsy-proven non-metastatic ascending-colon adenocarcinoma is treated with an oncologic right hemicolectomy + lymphadenectomy; final pathology determines stage and adjuvant chemotherapy.

questions.stem: A patient with a large benign-appearing polyp at the appendiceal orifice that may not be endoscopically removable asks about options. Which response best reflects proper informed consent?
options: 'Surgery is the only option; doing nothing isn't allowed'
'Options include expert endoscopic removal, surgery, or doing nothing; let's discuss benefits, risks, and your values'
questions.explain: Informed consent is a discussion of reasonable options (including no treatment), with benefits, material risks, and the patient's values — not a coerced choice or a mere signature.

questions.stem: A daily-NSAID user has sudden severe epigastric pain, a rigid abdomen with diffuse rebound, and free air under the diaphragm. Most appropriate management bundle?
options: Oral PPI and outpatient H. pylori testing
NPO, IV fluids, IV PPI, broad antibiotics, analgesia, and urgent surgical repair/source control
questions.explain: Perforated peptic ulcer with generalized peritonitis → resuscitate (NPO, IV fluids), IV PPI, broad antibiotics, analgesia, and urgent surgical source control. H. pylori/NSAID management comes after stabilization.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old develops marked colonic distension and obstipation after orthopedic surgery. CT shows diffuse colonic dilation with NO transition point or mass, no peritonitis. Diagnosis and initial management?
options: Mechanical LBO from cancer; immediate colectomy
Acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie); correct electrolytes, stop offending meds, mobilize, decompression, escalate to neostigmine/colonoscopic decompression if severe
questions.explain: Ogilvie syndrome (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction): colonic dilation without mechanical obstruction in a post-op/hospitalized patient. Start conservative care; escalate to neostigmine or colonoscopic decompression; surgery only for perforation/ischemia.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old scheduled for colorectal cancer surgery is anemic. Which strategy best aligns with perioperative blood management?
options: Autologous blood donation for most patients
Delay surgery until hemoglobin is 140 g/L
Preoperative IV iron when appropriate, plus patient-specific blood-conservation planning
Choose open surgery to reduce blood loss
questions.explain: Treat the anemia: preoperative IV iron for iron deficiency when time allows, with individualized blood conservation — rather than routine autologous donation or delaying cancer surgery for an arbitrary normal hemoglobin.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old has RUQ pain for 10 hours, fever, and a positive Murphy sign with leukocytosis. US shows gallbladder wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid around stones. Best management?
options: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy
questions.explain: This is acute cholecystitis (persistent cystic-duct obstruction). Manage with NPO, fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, and early cholecystectomy (percutaneous cholecystostomy if too unfit for surgery).

questions.stem: A patient with SBO has a tender, firm, irreducible groin lump with overlying skin discoloration and is febrile. What does this indicate and what is the management?
options: Incarcerated but viable hernia; prolonged manual reduction
questions.explain: A tender, irreducible hernia with skin changes and systemic signs suggests strangulation (compromised blood supply) — a surgical emergency. Do not attempt forceful reduction.

questions.stem: A stable, afebrile patient has RUQ discomfort, jaundice, cholestatic LFTs, and dilated bile ducts with a stone in the common bile duct on imaging. Best management?
options: Immediate cholecystectomy without addressing the duct
ERCP to clear the duct, then cholecystectomy
questions.explain: Choledocholithiasis (CBD stone) → ERCP to clear the duct, followed by cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence. ERCP addresses the duct; the gallbladder still needs removal.

questions.stem: A young woman has severe tearing pain with defecation and a streak of bright blood on the toilet paper; exam suggests a posterior midline fissure. Besides fiber and sitz baths, which medical therapy is appropriate?
options: Topical nitroglycerin or a calcium-channel blocker to relax the sphincter
questions.explain: Anal fissures are perpetuated by internal-sphincter spasm. Topical nitroglycerin or diltiazem/nifedipine relaxes the sphincter and improves anoderm blood flow; chronic refractory cases get botulinum toxin or sphincterotomy.

questions.stem: A patient has a painful, fluctuant, erythematous perianal swelling with fever. What is the definitive management?
questions.explain: A perianal abscess requires incision and drainage — antibiotics alone are inadequate for a drainable collection (added only for significant cellulitis, immunosuppression, or systemic illness).

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with iron-deficiency anemia and a change in bowel habits asks for a FIT test for screening. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Do a FIT; it is the right screening test
He needs diagnostic colonoscopy — symptoms/anemia are not 'screening'
questions.explain: Symptoms and iron-deficiency anemia require diagnostic colonoscopy, not a screening stool test. Hemorrhoids are a diagnosis of exclusion; colorectal cancer must be ruled out.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man has colon cancer. His family history: 3 relatives with colorectal/endometrial cancer across 2 generations, one diagnosed at 45. What does this suggest and what is the next step?
options: A hereditary syndrome (Lynch); genetics referral and counseling
questions.explain: The Amsterdam '3-2-1' pattern (3 relatives, 2 generations, 1 first-degree, one <50) and early-onset cancer suggest Lynch syndrome → genetics referral (and tumour MMR/MSI testing). This screens for hereditary risk; it doesn't replace counseling.

questions.stem: A patient with epigastric pain radiating to the back has a lipase 5× the upper limit of normal and gallstones on US, without cholangitis. What is the cornerstone of initial management?
options: Supportive care: aggressive IV fluids, analgesia, and early enteral nutrition as tolerated
questions.explain: Acute pancreatitis is managed supportively (fluids, analgesia, early enteral nutrition). ERCP is reserved for concurrent cholangitis/persistent obstruction; antibiotics are only for infected necrosis. Cholecystectomy is done during the same admission for mild gallstone pancreatitis.

questions.stem: A patient is admitted for major abdominal surgery. Which is part of standard perioperative VTE prevention when bleeding risk permits?
options: Pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) plus mechanical measures and early mobilization
questions.explain: Surgical VTE prevention combines pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/UFH when bleeding risk allows), mechanical measures, and early mobilization — not prolonged bed rest.

questions.stem: An elderly nursing-home resident has marked distension and a 'coffee-bean' / bent-inner-tube sign on abdominal x-ray, without peritonitis. What is the appropriate initial management of this large-bowel obstruction?
options: Endoscopic detorsion (sigmoid volvulus), with surgery to prevent recurrence
questions.explain: The coffee-bean sign indicates sigmoid volvulus. If there's no ischemia/peritonitis, endoscopic detorsion/decompression is the initial step; because recurrence is common, definitive prevention is surgical (sigmoidectomy).

cards.prompt: What is the first principle in any acute abdomen?
cards.answer: Stabilize while you diagnose — ABCs, IV access, fluids, analgesia, antiemetics, NPO, labs; an unstable patient may need resuscitation and source control before full diagnostic certainty.

cards.prompt: Contrast visceral and parietal abdominal pain.
cards.answer: Visceral pain is vague, poorly localized, and midline (autonomic); parietal pain is sharp, localized, worse with movement, and signals peritoneal irritation/peritonitis.

cards.prompt: Which first imaging test for biliary disease vs diverticulitis/obstruction?
cards.answer: Ultrasound first for RUQ/biliary disease; CT abdomen/pelvis for diverticulitis, obstruction, perforation, and undifferentiated stable acute abdomen.

cards.prompt: What are the three pillars of managing intra-abdominal sepsis?
cards.answer: Resuscitate (fluids, oxygen, pressors if needed), early broad-spectrum antibiotics (gram-negatives + anaerobes), and source control — antibiotics support but do not replace source control.

cards.prompt: Describe the classic appendicitis presentation.
cards.answer: Anorexia and periumbilical (visceral) pain that migrates to the RLQ (parietal) as peritoneal irritation develops, with low-grade fever and localized RLQ tenderness; treat with appendectomy.

cards.prompt: How does a perforated peptic ulcer present and what is the management?
cards.answer: Sudden severe epigastric pain with generalized peritonitis (± shoulder pain), free air under the diaphragm; manage with NPO, IV fluids, IV PPI, antibiotics, analgesia, and urgent surgical repair.

cards.prompt: How do biliary colic and acute cholecystitis differ?
cards.answer: Biliary colic = transient cystic-duct obstruction (episodic post-prandial pain, no fever, normal labs). Cholecystitis = persistent obstruction with inflammation (pain >6 h, fever, positive Murphy, leukocytosis).

cards.prompt: State Charcot triad and Reynolds pentad, and the treatment of cholangitis.
cards.answer: Charcot: fever, RUQ pain, jaundice. Reynolds adds hypotension and altered mental status. Treat with resuscitation, broad antibiotics, and urgent ERCP biliary decompression.

cards.prompt: When is ERCP indicated (and not)?
cards.answer: For CBD stones, ascending cholangitis, and persistent biliary obstruction — not for stones sitting only in the gallbladder (that needs cholecystectomy).

cards.prompt: Why CT and not colonoscopy in acute diverticulitis?
cards.answer: CT is the best acute test; colonoscopy is avoided acutely due to perforation risk. Colonoscopy is done after recovery because cancer can mimic diverticulitis.

cards.prompt: How is asymptomatic diverticulosis managed?
cards.answer: It is not diverticulitis — no antibiotics or surgery; fiber and lifestyle measures only.

cards.prompt: What are the most common causes of small-bowel obstruction?
cards.answer: Adhesions (prior surgery) and hernias; also Crohn stricture, malignancy, gallstone ileus, and intussusception. Always examine the groins and prior incisions.

cards.prompt: Which features of bowel obstruction mandate surgery?
cards.answer: Peritonitis, strangulation, closed-loop obstruction, ischemia, perforation/free air, unstable sepsis, cecal distension (impending perforation), or failed conservative management.

cards.prompt: Define reducible, incarcerated, and strangulated hernia.
cards.answer: Reducible: contents return to the abdomen. Incarcerated: cannot be reduced. Strangulated: blood supply compromised — a surgical emergency; don't forcefully reduce.

cards.prompt: State the internal hemorrhoid grades.
cards.answer: I: bleed without prolapse; II: prolapse that reduces spontaneously; III: prolapse needing manual reduction; IV: irreducible. Bands for I–III; surgery for IV/refractory.

cards.prompt: Why are hemorrhoids a 'diagnosis of exclusion'?
cards.answer: Iron-deficiency anemia, weight loss, or a change in bowel habits must not be attributed to hemorrhoids — colonoscopy is required to exclude colorectal cancer.

cards.prompt: Why isn't a symptomatic patient a candidate for screening tests?
cards.answer: Screening is for asymptomatic average-risk people; symptoms or iron-deficiency anemia require diagnostic colonoscopy. A positive stool test always needs colonoscopy.

cards.prompt: What is the staging workup after a colon cancer diagnosis?
cards.answer: Complete colonoscopy + biopsy, baseline CEA, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, bloodwork, and surgical-fitness assessment; rectal cancer also needs pelvic MRI and neoadjuvant planning.

cards.prompt: What does informed consent require?
cards.answer: A discussion of diagnosis, recommended treatment, reasonable alternatives (including no treatment), benefits, and material/patient-specific risks, with confirmation of understanding and voluntariness — not just a signature.

cards.prompt: How should preoperative iron-deficiency anemia be managed?
cards.answer: Identify and treat it — preoperative IV iron when time allows, with blood-conservation planning — rather than routine autologous donation or delaying cancer surgery for a normal hemoglobin.

cards.prompt: Periumbilical pain migrating to the RLQ is classic for:
First-line imaging for suspected gallbladder disease:
Definitive intervention for ascending cholangitis is:
Best acute diagnostic test for diverticulitis:
The most important cause of large-bowel obstruction to exclude is:
Ogilvie syndrome is characterized by:
Topical therapy for a chronic anal fissure works by:
Definitive management of a perianal abscess is:
The '3-2-1' family pattern suggests:
Surgical prophylactic antibiotics should be given:
options: Before the incision (within the appropriate window)

cards.prompt: Select features that should trigger urgent surgical assessment:
Select gallstone-related diagnoses:
Select bowel-obstruction findings that mandate surgery:
options: Stable partial SBO with normal lactate

cards.prompt: Select colorectal cancer risk factors:
Select evidence-based perioperative measures:
options: Preoperative IV iron for iron-deficiency anemia

cards.text: Charcot triad = fever, RUQ pain, {{jaundice}}; Reynolds pentad adds {{hypotension}} and {{altered mental status}}.
cards.explain: Reynolds pentad signals severe cholangitis needing urgent decompression.

cards.text: Hernia spectrum: reducible → {{incarcerated}} (won't reduce) → {{strangulated}} (blood supply compromised).
A grade {{II}} internal hemorrhoid prolapses but {{reduces spontaneously}}.
cards.explain: Grade III needs manual reduction; IV is irreducible.

cards.text: The Amsterdam pattern: {{3}} affected relatives across {{2}} generations, ≥1 first-degree, one diagnosis before age {{50}}.
cards.explain: Triggers consideration of Lynch syndrome and genetics referral.

cards.text: Acute pancreatitis: ≥2 of characteristic pain, lipase > {{3}}× upper limit, or {{imaging}}; main causes are gallstones and {{alcohol}}.
cards.explain: Management is supportive (fluids, analgesia, early enteral feeding).

cards.prompt: Order intra-abdominal sepsis management:
Order initial bowel-obstruction management:
items: Surgery if strangulation/peritonitis/closed loop or failed conservative care

cards.prompt: Order the gallstone spectrum by escalating obstruction:
Match the diagnosis to its best initial imaging:
Match the anorectal condition to its clue:
Match the obstruction type to a typical cause:
Match the drug to its surgical use:
What does painless jaundice with a palpable non-tender gallbladder suggest?
cards.answer: Malignant biliary obstruction (Courvoisier's sign — e.g. pancreatic head cancer), rather than gallstone disease, which is usually painful with a non-distensible fibrotic gallbladder.

cards.prompt: What must be excluded before giving neostigmine for Ogilvie syndrome, and what monitoring is needed?
cards.answer: Exclude mechanical obstruction and perforation; give in a monitored setting with atropine available because of bradycardia/bronchospasm risk.

cards.prompt: What is the feared complication of a colectomy that is material to consent?
cards.answer: Anastomotic leak — it can cause peritonitis/sepsis and is devastating, so it must be discussed (along with bleeding, SSI, ileus, VTE, and hernia) during consent.

guides.title: Week 31 · 1 Acute Abdomen: Approach & Imaging
guides.html:
Framework

Acute Abdomen: Approach & Imaging

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Stabilize-while-you-diagnose, localize and classify the pain, recognize peritonitis, and pick the right first imaging test.

Stabilize first, diagnose in parallel

ABCs, mental status, IV access, fluids, analgesia (don't withhold it to "preserve the exam"), antiemetics, NPO, broad labs. An unstable patient may need resuscitation and source control before diagnostic certainty.

Localize the pain

RegionOrgans
RUQGallbladder, liver, duodenum, right kidney, lung/heart
RLQAppendix, cecum, right ureter, ovary
LLQSigmoid, left ureter, ovary
EpigastriumStomach, duodenum, pancreas, aorta, heart

Classify the pain

  • Visceral: vague, poorly localized, midline, autonomic (early appendicitis = periumbilical).
  • Parietal: sharp, localized, worse with movement → peritonitis (e.g. migration to RLQ).
  • Referred: via shared neural pathways (biliary → right shoulder/scapula).
Recognize peritonitis & sepsis
Generalized peritonitis (rigidity, rebound), hypotension, altered LOC, high lactate, or hypoxemia → urgent surgical assessment + broad-spectrum antibiotics. Do not delay the surgeon for imaging in generalized peritonitis or refractory shock.

Pick imaging by suspected system

  • RUQ/biliary → ultrasound first. Diverticulitis / obstruction / undifferentiated stable acute abdomen → CT abdomen/pelvis. Appendicitis → US or CT by age/sex/pathway. Free perforation → upright CXR may show free air, but CT is often definitive in stable patients.
Resuscitate while you work up. Migrating periumbilical→RLQ pain = appendicitis. US for gallbladder, CT for almost everything else — but peritonitis/shock goes to the surgeon, not the scanner.

guides.title: Week 31 · 2 Intra-Abdominal Sepsis & Appendicitis
guides.html:
Source control

Intra-Abdominal Sepsis & Appendicitis

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why a contained infection turns catastrophic, the resuscitate-antibiotics-source-control bundle, and appendicitis and perforated ulcer.

Why appendicitis can become catastrophic

Local infection → systemic inflammatory response → vasodilation, capillary leak, myocardial depression → distributive (septic) shock, tissue hypoperfusion, lactate elevation, organ dysfunction. Patients can look only mildly ill then crash after perforation — reassess frequently.

The bundle: resuscitate → antibiotics → source control
1) Resuscitate (O₂, two large-bore IVs, crystalloid, Foley, repeat lactate; pressors if hypotensive after fluids). 2) Early broad-spectrum antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives + anaerobes (e.g. ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam). 3) Source control (appendectomy, abscess drainage, ulcer repair, ERCP for cholangitis) — antibiotics support but do not replace it.

Appendicitis

UncomplicatedComplicated/perforated
Anorexia, nausea, periumbilical→RLQ pain, low-grade fever, localized RLQ peritonitis; mild leukocytosisHigh fever, tachycardia, generalized peritonitis or abscess/phlegmon, sepsis/shock
Appendectomy + perioperative antibioticsResuscitate, broad antibiotics, urgent source control (a contained abscess may be drained with interval appendectomy)

Perforated peptic ulcer

Sudden severe epigastric pain → generalized peritonitis, ± shoulder pain (diaphragmatic irritation). Risk: NSAIDs, H. pylori, smoking. Upright CXR may show free air under the diaphragm; CT is sensitive. Manage: NPO, IV fluids, NG decompression, IV PPI, broad antibiotics, analgesia, urgent surgical repair; treat H. pylori and stop NSAIDs later.

Worsening vitals/mental status beat the original label — reassess. Antibiotics never replace source control. Sudden epigastric pain + free air + NSAIDs = perforated ulcer → OR.

guides.title: Week 31 · 3 Biliary Disease & Pancreatitis
guides.html:
Gallstones

Biliary Disease & Pancreatitis

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The five gallstone syndromes side by side, when ERCP is (and isn't) the answer, and gallstone pancreatitis.

Five gallstone syndromes

DiagnosisPictureTreatment
Biliary colicTransient cystic-duct obstruction: post-prandial RUQ pain to back/shoulder, resolves, no fever; normal labsElective lap cholecystectomy if symptomatic
Acute cholecystitisPersistent obstruction: RUQ pain >6 h, fever, +Murphy, leukocytosis; US wall thickening/pericholecystic fluidNPO, fluids, antibiotics, early cholecystectomy (cholecystostomy if unfit)
CholedocholithiasisCBD stone: jaundice, cholestatic LFTs, dilated ducts; may be afebrileERCP to clear the duct, then cholecystectomy
Ascending cholangitisCharcot triad (fever, RUQ pain, jaundice); Reynolds pentad adds hypotension + altered LOCResuscitate + antibiotics + urgent ERCP decompression
Gallstone pancreatitisEpigastric pain to back, ↑lipase, gallstone cause on USSupportive; ERCP if cholangitis/obstruction; cholecystectomy this admission if mild
ERCP is for ducts, not the gallbladder
ERCP treats CBD stones, cholangitis, and persistent biliary obstruction — not stones sitting only in the gallbladder. Antibiotics aren't needed for simple biliary colic, but are for cholecystitis and cholangitis. Painless jaundice + weight loss + a palpable non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier) suggests malignant obstruction, not stones.

Acute pancreatitis

Diagnose with ≥2 of: characteristic pain, lipase >3× upper limit, or imaging. Causes: gallstones and alcohol (most), plus hypertriglyceridemia, drugs, ERCP, hypercalcemia. Management is supportive: aggressive fluids, analgesia, early enteral nutrition as tolerated; antibiotics only for infected necrosis.

Walk the duct: colic (transient) → cholecystitis (inflamed gallbladder, +Murphy) → choledocholithiasis (CBD stone, jaundice) → cholangitis (infected duct, Charcot/Reynolds → ERCP). Pancreatitis is supportive care.

guides.title: Week 31 · 4 Diverticular Disease
guides.html:
Diverticula

Diverticular Disease

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Diverticulosis vs diverticulitis, why CT (not colonoscopy) is the acute test, and the complicated-disease pathways.

What diverticula are

Outpouchings of the colon wall — usually false diverticula herniating where the vasa recta pierce the muscle. Risk: low-fiber diet, obesity, inactivity, NSAIDs, smoking; prevalence rises sharply with age. Diverticulosis (asymptomatic pouches) is not diverticulitis and needs no antibiotics/surgery.

Diverticulitis

Pathophysiology resembles appendicitis: a fecalith obstructs a diverticulum → stasis, pressure, ischemia, inflammation, microperforation. LLQ pain + fever + bowel-habit change (left-sided "appendicitis").

Exam traps
CT abdomen/pelvis is the best acute test — colonoscopy is avoided acutely (perforation risk). Diverticulitis and colon cancer mimic each other, so arrange a follow-up colonoscopy after recovery to exclude cancer. Uncomplicated disease may be managed with bowel rest ± selective antibiotics by severity.

Complicated disease

ComplicationClue / management
AbscessPersistent fever, mass → IV antibiotics + percutaneous drainage if large
Fistula (colovesical)Pneumaturia/fecaluria → elective resection
Obstruction / strictureObstructive symptoms → elective resection
Free perforationGeneralized peritonitis/sepsis → resuscitate + urgent surgery (± stoma)
CT acutely, colonoscopy later (to rule out cancer). Asymptomatic diverticulosis needs nothing. Abscess → drain; perforation/peritonitis → OR.

guides.title: Week 31 · 5 Bowel Obstruction & Hernias
guides.html:
Obstruction

Bowel Obstruction & Hernias

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

SBO vs LBO vs pseudo-obstruction, the danger signs that mean the OR, and how a groin hernia can be the cause.

Recognize it

Crampy pain, nausea/vomiting, obstipation, distension. SBO: earlier vomiting; LBO: more distension/constipation. Initial management: NPO, IV fluids, electrolytes, NG tube if vomiting/distended, analgesia, and CT to define level/cause/complications.

TypeCommon causes
SBOAdhesions and hernias (most); Crohn stricture, malignancy, gallstone ileus, intussusception
LBOColorectal cancer, diverticular stricture, volvulus (sigmoid/cecal)
Ogilvie (pseudo-obstruction)Autonomic dysmotility — hospitalized/post-op/elderly; no mechanical transition point
Paralytic ileusPost-op, opioids, electrolyte disturbance
Danger signs → operate
Fever, tachycardia, continuous severe pain, peritonitis, leukocytosis, lactate/metabolic acidosis, closed-loop obstruction, pneumatosis, free air, or cecal distension (impending perforation, esp. with a competent ileocecal valve in LBO). Sigmoid volvulus = coffee-bean sign → often endoscopic detorsion first, but recurrence prevention is surgical. Ogilvie: conservative care + neostigmine/colonoscopic decompression; surgery only for perforation/ischemia.

Hernias

  • Always examine the groins and prior incisions in obstruction — a tender irreducible hernia can be the cause.
  • Reducible (returns) → incarcerated (won't reduce) → strangulated (compromised blood supply — surgical emergency).
  • Don't forcefully reduce if there is strangulation, peritonitis, toxicity, or skin changes.
Adhesions/hernias cause SBO; cancer/volvulus cause LBO. Stable simple SBO → bowel rest + NG + observe. Strangulation, closed loop, peritonitis, or free air → OR. Tender irreducible hernia = strangulation until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 31 · 6 Benign Anorectal Disease
guides.html:
Anorectal

Benign Anorectal Disease

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Hemorrhoid grades, the painful-vs-painless bleeding split, and the abscess/fistula/fissure trio.

Bleeding pattern recognition

FindingThink
Painful defecation + small bright bloodAnal fissure (posterior midline) until proven otherwise
Painless bright blood + prolapseInternal hemorrhoids (but rule out cancer/anemia)
Painful bluish perianal lumpThrombosed external hemorrhoid
Painful erythematous swelling ± feverPerianal abscess → incision & drainage
Drainage from a perianal opening after an abscessFistula-in-ano → surgical referral

Internal hemorrhoid grades

  • I bleed, no prolapse; II prolapse, reduces spontaneously; III needs manual reduction; IV irreducible.
  • Manage I–III with fiber/fluids/stool softeners and rubber-band ligation; grade IV/refractory → hemorrhoidectomy.
Hemorrhoids are a diagnosis of exclusion
Bleeding attributed to hemorrhoids must not explain iron-deficiency anemia, weight loss, or a change in bowel habits — these mandate colonoscopy to exclude colorectal cancer first.

Fissure, abscess, fistula

  • Anal fissure: fiber/fluids/sitz baths + topical nitroglycerin or a calcium-channel blocker (relax the sphincter); chronic refractory → botulinum toxin or lateral internal sphincterotomy.
  • Perianal abscess: incision and drainage — antibiotics alone are inadequate for a drainable abscess.
  • Fistula-in-ano: fistulotomy for simple low tracts; seton/flap/LIFT for complex tracts (preserve continence).
Painful bleeding = fissure; painless bleeding with prolapse = internal hemorrhoid. Drain the abscess (not just antibiotics). Never blame anemia on hemorrhoids without a scope.

guides.title: Week 31 · 7 Colorectal Cancer & Screening
guides.html:
CRC

Colorectal Cancer & Screening

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The adenoma-carcinoma sequence, screening vs diagnostic colonoscopy, the staging workup, and hereditary-risk recognition.

Biology & risk

Most CRC is sporadic via the adenoma-carcinoma sequence. Risk: age, male sex, Western diet, obesity, diabetes, inactivity, prior polyps/CRC, family history, IBD, and hereditary syndromes (Lynch, FAP). Physical activity is protective (~24% lower risk in the most vs least active).

Symptoms are not "screening"
Iron-deficiency anemia, change in bowel habits, weight loss, or rectal bleeding require diagnostic colonoscopy — bleeding hemorrhoids are a diagnosis of exclusion. Screening is for asymptomatic average-risk people.

Screening (average risk)

  • Stool tests (FIT/FOBT at program intervals) or structural tests (colonoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, CT colonography). A positive stool test requires colonoscopy.
  • Follow current local policy (some programs lowered the start age to 45).

Workup & treatment after diagnosis

  • Complete colonoscopy + biopsy (adenocarcinoma is the major type), baseline CEA, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging, bloodwork, surgical-fitness assessment. Rectal cancer also needs pelvic MRI and neoadjuvant planning.
  • Colon cancer: oncologic segmental colectomy + lymphadenectomy (e.g. right hemicolectomy for ascending tumours); adjuvant chemo by stage.
  • Rectal cancer: often neoadjuvant chemoradiation + total mesorectal excision; sphincter-preservation/stoma counseling.

Hereditary-risk recognition

The Amsterdam "3-2-1" pattern: 3 affected relatives across 2 generations, ≥1 first-degree relative, with one diagnosis before age 50 → consider genetics referral (Lynch). Early-onset CRC, many polyps, or synchronous tumours also trigger referral.

Adenoma-carcinoma sequence; activity protects. Symptoms → diagnostic colonoscopy (not screening). Stage with CEA + CT (+ pelvic MRI for rectal). 3-2-1 family pattern → think Lynch.

guides.title: Week 31 · 8 Perioperative Care
guides.html:
Around surgery

Perioperative Care

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Consent as a conversation, surgical-site-infection and VTE prevention, treating anemia before the OR, and ERAS.

Informed consent

Consent is a discussion, not a signature
Explain the diagnosis, recommended treatment, reasonable alternatives (including no treatment), benefits, material and patient-specific risks, then confirm understanding and voluntariness. Example: a large benign-appearing right-colon polyp not safely removed endoscopically → options are expert endoscopic removal, surgery, or doing nothing, compared on benefits/risks/values.

Surgical-site-infection prevention

  • Optimize glucose, nutrition, and smoking; prophylactic antibiotics before incision (e.g. cefazolin ± metronidazole for colorectal), re-dose in long/bloody cases; mechanical bowel prep + oral antibiotics for selected colorectal surgery; maintain normothermia and sterile technique.

VTE prophylaxis

  • Assess thrombosis vs bleeding risk; use mechanical + pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/enoxaparin or UFH) when indicated, plus early mobilization.

Anemia & transfusion

Treat anemia, don't just transfuse
The taught strategy is preoperative IV iron when iron-deficient and time allows, with patient-specific blood-conservation planning — not routine autologous donation and not delaying cancer surgery until an arbitrary "normal" hemoglobin (e.g. 140).

ERAS & cardiac risk

  • ERAS: minimize prolonged fasting, carbohydrate drinks when allowed, euvolemia, multimodal analgesia (limit opioids), early feeding/mobilization, avoid unnecessary tubes/drains.
  • Cardiac/anesthesia: assess functional capacity and comorbidity; manage antiplatelets/prior stents; order tests only if they will change management.
Consent = options + risks + values. Prophylactic antibiotics before incision, VTE prophylaxis + early mobilization, and IV iron for preoperative anemia. Don't order reflexive preoperative tests.

guides.title: Week 31 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen

Week 31 · GI III — Surgery & Acute Abdomen
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Septic shock from an abdominal source → resuscitate + early broad antibiotics + urgent SOURCE CONTROL (don't wait for perfect imaging)
Ascending cholangitis (Charcot/Reynolds) → resuscitate + antibiotics + urgent ERCP decompression
Perforated peptic ulcer (free air, NSAIDs) → NPO, IV PPI, antibiotics, urgent surgical repair
Strangulated hernia / closed-loop or ischemic obstruction → OR (don't forcefully reduce a strangulated hernia)
Generalized peritonitis / refractory shock → surgeon now, not the CT scanner
IDA or change in bowel habit blamed on hemorrhoids → colonoscopy to exclude colorectal cancer

Approach Approach

Stabilize while diagnosing; give analgesia
Visceral pain vague, midline (early appendicitis)
Parietal pain localized → peritonitis
US first RUQ / gallbladder
CT first diverticulitis, obstruction, perforation
Peritonitis/shock surgeon before scanner

Sepsis & appendicitis Sepsis

Bundle resuscitate → antibiotics → source control
Antibiotics gram-neg + anaerobe (ceftriaxone+metro)
Appendicitis periumbilical → RLQ; appendectomy
Perforated ulcer free air; NPO, IV PPI, OR
Lactate tissue hypoperfusion — high risk

Biliary & pancreatitis Biliary

Biliary colic transient; elective cholecystectomy
Cholecystitis >6 h, fever, +Murphy; early cholecystectomy
Choledocholithiasis CBD stone → ERCP then cholecystectomy
Cholangitis Charcot/Reynolds → urgent ERCP
ERCP ducts, not the gallbladder
Pancreatitis supportive; lipase >3×

Diverticulitis Diverticular

Picture LLQ pain + fever
Acute test CT (not colonoscopy)
After recovery colonoscopy to exclude cancer
Diverticulosis no antibiotics/surgery
Abscess drain; perforation → OR

Obstruction & hernias Obstruction

SBO adhesions / hernias
LBO cancer / volvulus
Ogilvie no transition point; neostigmine
Operate strangulation, closed loop, ischemia, free air
Sigmoid volvulus coffee-bean; endoscopic detorsion
Strangulated hernia tender, irreducible → OR

Anorectal Anorectal

Painful bleeding fissure
Painless bleeding + prolapse internal hemorrhoid
Grades I bleed, II spont reduce, III manual, IV irreducible
Abscess incision & drainage
Fissure topical nitroglycerin / CCB
Anemia never blame hemorrhoids → scope

CRC & perioperative CRC/Periop

Adenoma-carcinoma sporadic majority; activity protects
Symptoms diagnostic colonoscopy (not screening)
Staging CEA + CT (+ pelvic MRI for rectal)
3-2-1 Lynch → genetics referral
Consent options + risks + values
Preop anemia IV iron; VTE prophylaxis + mobilize
